scenario: A Sequential Multi-Instance Subprocess iterates over customers. Each instance does account-validation. The team wants only one instance at a time because the back-end API has a 1-request-per-second limit.
question: Which Multi-Instance configuration fits?

options.text: Parallel Multi-Instance with loopCardinality equal to 1 creates exactly one instance in total â€” loopCardinality specifies the total number of instances to create, not the concurrency level. This misinterprets the attribute: one parallel instance means one instance runs once, not that the full customer list is processed one at a time. To iterate over the customer collection one-at-a-time, Sequential mode with inputCollection is the correct configuration, not Parallel mode with a cardinality of one.

Parallel Multi-Instance combined with sleep(1) in the worker is a fragile timing hack. Worker sleep introduces artificial delay but does not guarantee a 1-request-per-second rate limit because all parallel instances activate and acquire jobs roughly simultaneously regardless of individual worker sleep times. Sleep also wastes thread resources. Sequential Multi-Instance is the correct architecture â€” it serialises iteration at the BPMN level, enforcing one active instance at a time without any worker-side timing workarounds.

Sequential Multi-Instance with inputCollection equal to customers and inputElement equal to customer runs exactly one subprocess instance at a time â€” each instance completes before the next starts. Sequential mode inherently satisfies a 1-request-per-second rate limit because only one instance runs concurrently. As long as each instance takes approximately 1 second to complete, the downstream API receives at most one request per second. This is the canonical Camunda 8 configuration for serialised iteration.

Multi-Instance can serve as a rate-limiting mechanism by choosing Sequential mode, which runs one instance at a time naturally. The claim that Multi-Instance cannot be rate-limited reflects a misunderstanding of Sequential versus Parallel modes. Sequential mode is precisely designed for serialised iteration. For a simple 1-per-second rate limit, Sequential MI combined with each instance taking approximately one second of processing time provides adequate rate control without external queue infrastructure. ###

a.text: Incorrect. Sequential mode runs instances one at a time -- natural fit for serialised work. The 1-RPS limit is satisfied if each instance completes in ≥ 1 sec.

b.text: Incorrect. loopCardinality = 1 means one instance total, not rate-limited.

c.text: Correct. Combining Parallel with worker sleeps creates fragile timing; sequential mode is canonical.

d.text: Incorrect. Sequential mode addresses this natively.

explanation: Combining Parallel with worker sleeps creates fragile timing; sequential mode is canonical.
